sound_type: confrontation tree-shaking display: bear seizes small tree, shakes with full upper-body force; simultaneous wood-crack, branch-snap, foliage crash, and explosive huff vocalizations; stationary display; audible 50-100 meters; high-arousal defensive confrontation
ecosystem: Confrontation context -- defensive or territorial assertion at close range, in response to human or large predator proximity, or intraspecific competition; forest interior and edge
season: Resident (year-round at low elevations; confrontation displays occur whenever bear is surprised or challenged)
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). Common and widespread throughout Pacific Northwest. Population approximately 25,000-30,000 in Washington state alone. Not threatened.
milestone: SECOND URSIDAE ENTRY. Completes the Ursidae pair with SPS-304 (Grizzly Bear threat-charge, E=8). Grizzly = kinetic locomotion toward listener (E=8); Black Bear = stationary display at fixed point (E=7). Both bear taxa of the Pacific Northwest documented at appropriate relative energies.

coordinates.description: Pacific Northwest distribution: common throughout western Washington and Oregon, Cascade Range, Olympic Peninsula, Klamath Mountains. Habitat generalist. Low-elevation populations may be year-round active; high-elevation populations hibernate.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Combined mechanical and vocal: tree-shaking produces wood-crack (structural), branch-snap (fracture), foliage crash (secondary); huff is explosive nasal exhalation (vocal warning component). Multiple simultaneous acoustic sources.
acoustic_data.mechanics: Bear seizes tree trunk or major branch with forepaws and shakes with full upper-body strength. Force sufficient to fracture small branches and partially crack main trunk. Rapid, forceful, sustained for seconds to tens of seconds.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: Broadband mechanical sounds (wood-crack, snap, crash): energy across 100-5,000 Hz. Huff: low-frequency broadband exhalation, 100-1,000 Hz primary. Combined profile is complex and broadband.
acoustic_data.duration: Display lasts 5-30 seconds typically; may repeat. Huff vocalizations rapid in succession at high arousal.
acoustic_data.e7_calibration: E=7: stationary confrontational display at high force. Below Grizzly charge (E=8, kinetic locomotion). Above standard bear vocalization (E=5-6). The tree-shaking display is E=7 because it is maximum acoustic output from a stationary position.

nodes.title: Ursidae Pair: 304 Grizzly (E=8) + 305 Black Bear (E=7)
nodes.description: Both Pacific Northwest bear taxa documented in consecutive degrees at appropriate relative energies. Grizzly charge = kinetic, locomotive, coming toward listener (E=8). Black Bear tree-shaking = stationary, displaying at fixed point (E=7). The energy difference is the difference between moving at the threat and demonstrating at the threat.

nodes.title: Black Bear Confrontation as Communication
nodes.description: Tree-shaking is defensive confrontation, not predatory aggression. The bear communicates: I am here, I am large, I am capable, give me space. The display is a message, not an attack. Stationary intensity: maximum acoustic output to assert presence without motion.

cross_references.relationship: Consecutive Ursidae entries. E=8 vs. E=7. Kinetic vs. stationary. The family pair.
E=9 SPS maximum three degrees later. Both from old-growth forest contexts adjacent to Aberdeen-area timber country.
E=7 + E=7 = E=14. Perfect equilibrium. Both are confrontational assertions at a fixed point. Stationary intensity on both sides.
